topic_or_source: The United Kingdom announced a default midnight-to-6 a.m. social-media curfew for 16- and 17-year-olds, although teenagers will be able to override the setting.

paragraphs: On July 15, 2026, the United Kingdom announced a new default social-media setting for teenagers aged 16 and 17.
Under the plan, social-media use will be restricted by default from midnight until 6 a.m.
The hours begin at midnight and end at 6 a.m. The default period covers six hours of the night.
However, this is not an absolute overnight ban. Teenagers in the target age group will be able to override the setting themselves.
In other words, the restriction will be turned on as the normal choice, but a user can choose to change it.
The government also plans to disable or limit certain addictive features by default. This means the nighttime setting is not the only planned change.
The policy follows a government pilot involving more than 300 teenagers and parents.
The announcement is also part of a broader British plan to restrict social-media access for children under 16.

subsections.heading: How the Default Works
paragraphs: The key word is default. The midnight-to-6 a.m. restriction is the starting setting for 16- and 17-year-olds, rather than a rule that cannot be changed.
An override means the user can choose not to keep the default restriction. The policy therefore does not prevent every 16- or 17-year-old from using social media overnight.
This difference is important: a default restriction and a complete ban are not the same policy.

subsections.heading: A Wider Plan
paragraphs: The nighttime setting is linked to a larger government plan. That plan is intended to restrict social-media access for children under 16.
The July 15 announcement focuses on people aged 16 and 17, while the broader plan concerns younger children. These are two different age groups within the wider policy discussion.
The government pilot included more than 300 teenagers and parents. The announcement follows that pilot.
Both teenagers and parents took part in the pilot. The wider plan and the default setting are connected, but they refer to different age groups.

sections.heading: In One Line
paragraphs: The United Kingdom announced a default midnight-to-6 a.m. social-media restriction for 16- and 17-year-olds that users can override.
Certain addictive features are also planned to be disabled or limited by default.
This is one part of a broader plan about social-media access for children under 16.

chunks:
A Nighttime Social-Media Setting

On July 15, 2026, the United Kingdom announced a new default social-media setting for teenagers aged 16 and 17.

Under the plan, social-media use will be restricted by default from midnight until 6 a.m.

The hours begin at midnight and end at 6 a.m. The default period covers six hours of the night.

However, this is not an absolute overnight ban. Teenagers in the target age group will be able to override the setting themselves.

In other words, the restriction will be turned on as the normal choice, but a user can choose to change it.

The government also plans to disable or limit certain addictive features by default. This means the nighttime setting is not the only planned change.

The policy follows a government pilot involving more than 300 teenagers and parents.

The announcement is also part of a broader British plan to restrict social-media access for children under 16.

Today's Nighttime Access Points

Point One

How the Default Works

The key word is default. The midnight-to-6 a.m. restriction is the starting setting for 16- and 17-year-olds, rather than a rule that cannot be changed.

An override means the user can choose not to keep the default restriction. The policy therefore does not prevent every 16- or 17-year-old from using social media overnight.

This difference is important: a default restriction and a complete ban are not the same policy.

Point Two

A Wider Plan

The nighttime setting is linked to a larger government plan. That plan is intended to restrict social-media access for children under 16.

The July 15 announcement focuses on people aged 16 and 17, while the broader plan concerns younger children. These are two different age groups within the wider policy discussion.

The government pilot included more than 300 teenagers and parents. The announcement follows that pilot.

Both teenagers and parents took part in the pilot. The wider plan and the default setting are connected, but they refer to different age groups.

In One Line

The United Kingdom announced a default midnight-to-6 a.m. social-media restriction for 16- and 17-year-olds that users can override.

Certain addictive features are also planned to be disabled or limited by default.

This is one part of a broader plan about social-media access for children under 16.
plan.full_text:
A Nighttime Social-Media Setting

On July 15, 2026, the United Kingdom announced a new default social-media setting for teenagers aged 16 and 17.

Under the plan, social-media use will be restricted by default from midnight until 6 a.m.

The hours begin at midnight and end at 6 a.m. The default period covers six hours of the night.

However, this is not an absolute overnight ban. Teenagers in the target age group will be able to override the setting themselves.

In other words, the restriction will be turned on as the normal choice, but a user can choose to change it.

The government also plans to disable or limit certain addictive features by default. This means the nighttime setting is not the only planned change.

The policy follows a government pilot involving more than 300 teenagers and parents.

The announcement is also part of a broader British plan to restrict social-media access for children under 16.

Today's Nighttime Access Points

Point One

How the Default Works

The key word is default. The midnight-to-6 a.m. restriction is the starting setting for 16- and 17-year-olds, rather than a rule that cannot be changed.

An override means the user can choose not to keep the default restriction. The policy therefore does not prevent every 16- or 17-year-old from using social media overnight.

This difference is important: a default restriction and a complete ban are not the same policy.

Point Two

A Wider Plan

The nighttime setting is linked to a larger government plan. That plan is intended to restrict social-media access for children under 16.

The July 15 announcement focuses on people aged 16 and 17, while the broader plan concerns younger children. These are two different age groups within the wider policy discussion.

The government pilot included more than 300 teenagers and parents. The announcement follows that pilot.

Both teenagers and parents took part in the pilot. The wider plan and the default setting are connected, but they refer to different age groups.

In One Line

The United Kingdom announced a default midnight-to-6 a.m. social-media restriction for 16- and 17-year-olds that users can override.

Certain addictive features are also planned to be disabled or limited by default.

This is one part of a broader plan about social-media access for children under 16.

embedded_classified.expected_text:
A Nighttime Social-Media Setting

On July 15, 2026, the United Kingdom announced a new default social-media setting for teenagers aged 16 and 17.

Under the plan, social-media use will be restricted by default from midnight until 6 a.m.

The hours begin at midnight and end at 6 a.m. The default period covers six hours of the night.

However, this is not an absolute overnight ban. Teenagers in the target age group will be able to override the setting themselves.

In other words, the restriction will be turned on as the normal choice, but a user can choose to change it.

The government also plans to disable or limit certain addictive features by default. This means the nighttime setting is not the only planned change.

The policy follows a government pilot involving more than 300 teenagers and parents.

The announcement is also part of a broader British plan to restrict social-media access for children under 16.

Today's Nighttime Access Points

Point One

How the Default Works

The key word is default. The midnight-to-6 a.m. restriction is the starting setting for 16- and 17-year-olds, rather than a rule that cannot be changed.

An override means the user can choose not to keep the default restriction. The policy therefore does not prevent every 16- or 17-year-old from using social media overnight.

This difference is important: a default restriction and a complete ban are not the same policy.

Point Two

A Wider Plan

The nighttime setting is linked to a larger government plan. That plan is intended to restrict social-media access for children under 16.

The July 15 announcement focuses on people aged 16 and 17, while the broader plan concerns younger children. These are two different age groups within the wider policy discussion.

The government pilot included more than 300 teenagers and parents. The announcement follows that pilot.

Both teenagers and parents took part in the pilot. The wider plan and the default setting are connected, but they refer to different age groups.

In One Line

The United Kingdom announced a default midnight-to-6 a.m. social-media restriction for 16- and 17-year-olds that users can override.

Certain addictive features are also planned to be disabled or limited by default.

This is one part of a broader plan about social-media access for children under 16.
